talk_title: Gut Microeukaryotes, Endogenous Viruses, and Cancer

models.name: ZOE PREDICT studies
models.genotype_or_key_features: n≈400,000 individuals across 5 cohorts, multi-omic data

MOA_summary: Nguyen described population-scale analyses linking gut microeukaryote Blastocystis prevalence to plant-based diets, favorable cardiometabolic markers, lower adiposity, and reduced odds of colorectal cancer, and presented trans-kingdom dysbiosis in MASLD driven by oral bacteria, acylcarnitine metabolites, and endogenous viruses that may modulate bacterial communities via predator-prey dynamics.
key_findings: Blastocystis prevalence exceeds 40% in several under‑studied regions (e.g., Tanzania, Fiji) and is higher in adults than children, suggesting non‑vertical transmission.
Higher adherence to plant‑based diets correlates with increased Blastocystis carriage (median AUC ~0.85 for bacterial composition predicting Blastocystis).
Blastocystis‑positive individuals show improved fasting/postprandial triglycerides, higher HDL, lower GlicA, and a 6% decrease in odds of carriage per BMI point increase.
Case‑control analysis shows a 29% lower odds of Blastocystis in colorectal cancer and IBD samples versus healthy controls.
In a 1,100‑person dietary RCT, Blastocystis prevalence modestly expanded in participants achieving weight‑loss goals on high‑quality diets.
MASLD cases exhibit enrichment of oral‑typical bacteria (e.g., Streptococcus spp.) distinct from obesity‑associated signatures.
Acylcarnitines are the most enriched metabolite class in MASLD, with chain‑length–specific associations to microbial taxa.
Endogenous viruses, especially unclassified taxa and Punavirus P1, differentiate MASLD subtypes; MASLD‑associated viruses have larger genomes, higher GC, and more ORFs.
Computational phage‑host predictions suggest MASLD‑enriched viruses target and kill their bacterial hosts, indicating predator‑prey dynamics.
open_questions: What mechanistic pathways link Blastocystis colonization to cardiometabolic improvements?
How do specific oral bacteria and viral predators interact to shape MASLD microbiome ecology?
Can targeted modulation of identified viruses or bacteria alter MASLD progression?

talk_title: Population‑scale EBV Persistence and Host Genetics

targets.dependency_context: latent EBV DNA in peripheral B cells

biomarkers.name: EBV DNA load in whole‑genome sequencing
biomarkers.finding: ≈10% of individuals show high EBV DNA levels (right‑hand tail) versus ~90% seropositive

models.name: UK Biobank
models.genotype_or_key_features: ≈900,000 participants, whole‑genome sequencing, EBV serology in 10,000

models.name: GTEx peripheral blood
models.genotype_or_key_features: RNA‑seq and WGS data for EBV DNA/RNA comparison

clinical_data.metric: GWAS loci associated with EBV persistence
clinical_data.population: high EBV DNA carriers vs low carriers

MOA_summary: La Roe demonstrated that latent EBV DNA can be quantified from existing whole‑genome sequencing data, identified a high‑EBV‑load subpopulation (~10%) and linked host genetic variation at 45 loci—many in antigen presentation and B‑cell pathways—to persistence, which in turn associates with autoimmune, respiratory, and cancer phenotypes, and provided preliminary evidence of geographic strain variation.
key_findings: Approximately 10% of individuals in UK Biobank and All of Us have high EBV DNA levels detectable in blood WGS, despite ~90% seropositivity.
Masking low‑complexity repetitive EBV regions is essential; otherwise 40% of samples would be mis‑classified.
GWAS of 400,000 European‑ancestry participants identified 45 independent loci (antigen presentation, B‑cell transcription factors) associated with EBV persistence.
Genetic risk for EBV persistence overlaps with risk loci for systemic lupus, type 1 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.
High‑EBV carriers show increased incidence of HIV infection, rheumatoid arthritis, COPD, lung cancer, and lymphomas across health‑record ICD‑10 data.
EBV strain analysis reveals 31 known variants; four novel variants are enriched in high‑EBV carriers and are being experimentally followed up.
Geographic comparison shows concordant EBV allele frequencies between UK and US cohorts, suggesting shared strain circulation.
open_questions: Which specific host genetic variants drive differential EBV latency control?
Do the four novel EBV variants directly influence persistence or disease risk?
Can interventions targeting EBV latency reduce associated autoimmune or cancer risks?

talk_title: Cancer Mycobiome: Truth, Myth, and Current Data

biomarkers.name: Candida albicans DNA/RNA in tumor tissue
biomarkers.finding: Detected in colorectal, stomach, head‑and‑neck, and esophageal cancers; absent in brain tumors

models.name: TCGA pan‑cancer cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 30+ cancer types, DNA‑seq and RNA‑seq data

models.name: Collected tumor biopsies (CRC, stomach, etc.)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Isolation of Candida strains, strain sequencing

models.name: AOM/DSS colon cancer model
models.genotype_or_key_features: Mice colonized with C. albicans vs C. glabrata

MOA_summary: Iliev highlighted that tumor‑associated fungi, especially Candida albicans, are present in the mucosal layer of gastrointestinal tumors, where strain‑specific production of the peptide toxin candidalysin induces IL‑17/IL‑1 inflammation and promotes tumorigenesis; antifungal fluconazole clears gut Candida and restores beneficial bacterial diversity and butyrate production, suggesting a causal role for the mycobiome in cancer progression.
key_findings: Fungal DNA (Candida albicans) is detectable in multiple GI cancers (CRC, stomach, esophageal, head‑and‑neck) but not in brain tumors.
Strain‑level analysis shows diverse C. albicans isolates with variable hyphal formation and candidalysin production.
In AOM/DSS mouse models, C. albicans colonization aggravates tumor development, whereas C. glabrata does not.
Fluconazole treatment clears both oral and gut Candida, whereas oral nystatin only clears oral colonization.
Systemic antifungal therapy restores bacterial diversity, notably increasing butyrate‑producing Faecalibacterium prausnitzii and Roseburia intestinalis, and raises fecal butyrate levels.
Candida‑positive tumors exhibit elevated IL‑17 and IL‑1 cytokine signatures linked to candidalysin activity.
Fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) that enriches bacterial communities can displace Candida, reducing mucosal inflammation.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: One patient did not respond to fluconazole due to a resistant Candida strain.
Oral nystatin fails to affect gut Candida, highlighting compartment‑specific drug efficacy.

combination_strategies.rationale: Clear gut Candida to restore bacterial diversity and reduce IL‑17 inflammation
combination_strategies.phenotype: Increased butyrate‑producing bacteria and fecal butyrate; tumor‑associated inflammation reduced

combination_strategies.rationale: Introduce a robust bacterial community to outcompete Candida and restore ecosystem balance
combination_strategies.phenotype: Candida depletion and recovery of microbial diversity in ulcerative colitis patients at high CRC risk

open_questions: Which specific candidalysin‑dependent pathways drive IL‑17–mediated tumor promotion?
Do particular C. albicans strains correlate with clinical outcomes in cancer patients?
Can combined antifungal and microbiome‑restorative therapies synergistically prevent tumor progression?
